purpose: Creates reusable components or functions that work with a variety of types rather than a single one.
syntax: function identity<T>(arg: T): T { return arg; }
proTip: Use 'Generic Constraints' (`<T extends K>`) to ensure the passed type has specific properties (e.g., an 'id' property).
commonMistake: Using 'any' instead of a generic, which removes all type safety and defeats the purpose of TypeScript.

purpose: Union (|) allows a value to be one of several types; Intersection (&) combines multiple types into one.
syntax: type Status = 'success' | 'error'; type UserWithRole = User & AdminRole;
proTip: Use Union types for 'state' variables (e.g., 'loading' | 'success' | 'error') to prevent invalid states like { loading: true, error: 'Something went wrong' }.
commonMistake: Over-using Intersection types, which can create complex, hard-to-debug type requirements.

purpose: Functions or checks that tell TypeScript exactly what type a variable is within a specific block of code.
syntax: if (typeof val === 'string') { ... } or if (val instanceof User) { ... }
example: function isString(val: any): val is string { return typeof val === 'string'; }
proTip: Create a custom Type Guard function (using the `val is Type` return signature) to keep your business logic clean and separated from type checks.
commonMistake: Using type assertions (`as string`) instead of narrowing. Assertions 'lie' to the compiler and can cause runtime crashes.

purpose: Creates a new type by transforming properties of an existing type.
syntax: type Readonly<T> = { readonly [P in keyof T]: T[P]; };
example: type UserPreview = { [K in keyof User]: string }; // Turns all User props into strings
proTip: Use Mapped Types to build powerful utility types that automate repetitive type definitions across a large project.
commonMistake: Trying to use Mapped Types for logic that should actually be handled by simple Interfaces.

purpose: Types that change their result based on a condition (Ternary for types).
example: type Flatten<T> = T extends any[] ? T[number] : T;
proTip: Conditional types are the foundation of advanced TypeScript libraries (like Zod or Prisma) for creating highly dynamic type systems.
commonMistake: Creating overly complex nested conditionals that make the type error messages impossible to read.

purpose: Built-in helpers to transform types quickly without writing custom mapped types.
syntax: Partial<T>, Pick<T, K>, Omit<T, K>, Record<K, T>
example: const updateUserInfo: Partial<User> = { name: 'Bob' }; // Only name is required
proTip: Use `Pick` and `Omit` to create 'View-specific' types (e.g., a UserProfile type that omits the password field).
commonMistake: Using `any` when a `Partial<T>` or `Record<string, any>` would have provided better type safety.

purpose: Allows creating types based on string patterns.
proTip: Use these for creating strictly typed CSS classes or API route paths to catch typos during development.
commonMistake: Over-complicating string types to the point where they are more confusing than a simple string.

purpose: Defines a contract for what a class should look like, with different levels of implementation.
syntax: abstract class Base { abstract move(): void; }
example: class Dog extends Animal { move() { console.log('Walk'); } }
proTip: Use an Interface for simple data shapes; use an Abstract Class when you want to provide some shared logic while forcing children to implement specific methods.
commonMistake: Using an Abstract Class when a simple Interface would suffice, adding unnecessary overhead to the compiled JS.
